results.title: Cross-chain voucher replay allows draining channels across Tempo networks
results.reasoning: The described vulnerability concerns cross-chain replay of vouchers in settle() due to missing chainId/domain binding in the EIP-712 voucher verification. The audit report does not flag this issue. The only related item (#6) addresses cross-chain replay for the cooperative close payer signature in close(), not for vouchers in settle(). These are different code paths and require different fixes. Fixing #6 (adding chainId/address(this) to the close hash) would not remediate the missing chainId/domain in voucher verification. Therefore, the specific voucher replay vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Cumulative amount underflow in close() allows draining channel deposits
results.reasoning: The described issue is an underflow in close() when voucher.cumulativeAmount < channel.settled, requiring an explicit lower-bound check and causing potential erroneous payouts. The audit’s closest item (#8) only flags an incorrect upper-bound validation (delta <= deposit vs deposit - settled) and treats arithmetic underflows as implicit protection, not as an exploitable flaw. It does not identify or recommend adding the critical check voucher.cumulativeAmount >= channel.settled. Other close()-related findings (missing expiry/nonce checks, lack of payee signature, grace-period bypass, stale state) are different mechanisms. Fixing the audit’s issue would not necessarily fix the underflow vulnerability described; thus, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Authorized signer validation bypass via zero address signature recovery
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit finding #1 describes the exact same flaw: openChannel allows authorizedSigner = address(0), and because ecrecover/recover returns address(0) for invalid signatures, the signer check in settle (and also in close) is bypassed, allowing anyone to settle/drain the channel. It references the same code path (openChannel storing zero signer; settle/close comparing recovered signer) and the same root cause and fix (validate authorizedSigner != address(0), optionally guard against zero recovered signer). Fixing the audit issue would fix the described vulnerability.
